sourceSections: The Problem of "Babysitting" AI Agents and Cloud Code Prerequisites
Rethinking Software Tooling for AI Agents
Verification Loops: Teaching Cloud to Check Its Own Work
Practical Implementation: Building Self-Improving Verification Skills (with Demo)
Scaling Agent Operations with Multi-Cloud Strategies
Automating Routine Tasks with Background Loops and Routines

sections.lead: Sid Bisaria addresses the inefficiency of monitoring AI agents, offering advanced Cloud Code strategies to better manage them and reclaim developer time.

bullets.label: Problem Highlighted
bullets.text: Sid Bisaria highlights the inefficient time developers spend monitoring and QA testing AI agents.

bullets.label: Talk Level
bullets.text: This is an "advanced Cloud Code talk" (301 level) requiring specific prerequisites for optimal understanding.

bullets.label: Tool Prerequisites
bullets.text: Users need a high-quality Cloud MD and essential developer tools like Slack, Asana, and Datadog connected.

bullets.label: Environment Setup
bullets.text: A remote environment on Cloud Code Web must be set up to decouple compute from the local machine.

sections.lead: Software tooling, traditionally for humans, needs rethinking as AI agents now write a significant portion of code.

bullets.label: Tooling Shift
bullets.text: Traditional software tooling was built primarily for human developers, but AI agents now write a significant portion of code.

bullets.label: Agent Needs
bullets.text: While tools like linters are beneficial, human developers make assumptions about tooling that agents don't share.

bullets.label: Key Question
bullets.text: The crucial question is what an AI agent needs from a codebase that a human takes for granted.

bullets.label: Core Concepts
bullets.text: The presentation will outline three core concepts to address this: verification, multi-clouding, and background loops.

sections.lead: Cloud can verify its own work by adopting human development processes through autonomous "verification loops."

bullets.label: Verification Loop
bullets.text: Cloud can adopt the human playbook of designing, building, running tests, checking side effects, and finally deploying code.

bullets.label: Autonomous Circuit
bullets.text: A "loop" is an autonomous circuit where Cloud writes code, checks for failures, debugs, and reiterates until a success state is achieved.

bullets.label: Verification Scope
bullets.text: Verification can apply to various aspects, including UX, backend, and end-to-end functionality.

bullets.label: UX Implementation
bullets.text: Concrete UX verification involves running an application, enabling Cloud via browser control tools like Chrome MCP, proving functionality with screenshots, and dynamically unblocking issues like authentication.

sections.lead: Verification "skills" can be self-improving, allowing agents to autonomously verify new features, as demonstrated in a live demo.

bullets.label: Skills Packaging
bullets.text: Verification loops can be packaged and distributed using "skills," which store arbitrary context about a topic.

bullets.label: Self-Improving
bullets.text: Skills can be made "self-improving" by including instructions for the agent to update them whenever it overcomes a blocker.

bullets.label: Demo Application
bullets.text: A live demo showcased this process using a Monkey Type application where Cloud spun up a dev server.

bullets.label: Agent Interaction
bullets.text: Cloud used the Chrome MCP to interact with the frontend, performing actions like typing and changing settings.

bullets.label: Feature Verification
bullets.text: Cloud implemented a new feature (confetti on mistype), debugged lint errors, and confirmed functionality using the newly created verification skill.

sections.lead: Multi-cloud strategies offer tools to manage multiple AI agent instances, scaling operations while protecting human attention.

bullets.label: Desktop App
bullets.text: The Cloud Code desktop app provides a graphical user interface for managing sessions across all surfaces, allowing users to pin, rename, and color sessions.

bullets.label: Terminal View
bullets.text: "Cloud Agents" offers a similar terminal-based view, prioritizing sessions that require immediate attention.

bullets.label: Decoupled Compute
bullets.text: Running Cloud Code on the web decouples agent compute from your local laptop, enabling continuous operation regardless of your device's status.

bullets.label: Remote Control
bullets.text: "Remote control" allows users to monitor and interact with any agent session from their phone, receiving notifications when input is needed.

sections.lead: Cloud Code's `/loop` command and "routines" automate "bookkeeping" tasks, freeing developers for higher-value activities.

bullets.label: Routine Tasks
bullets.text: Software engineers spend significant time on "bookkeeping" tasks such as babysitting PRs, updating documentation, triaging feedback, and maintaining CI health.

bullets.label: Loop Command
bullets.text: The `/loop` command in Cloud Code allows a prompt to be run at a specified interval, enabling autonomous task management (e.g., `/loop 10 minutes and babysit my open PRs`).

bullets.label: Routines Functionality
bullets.text: "Routines" are remote loops, managed via the web or desktop app, with time-based or event-based triggers.

bullets.label: Full Automation
bullets.text: Routines can spawn new Cloud Code sessions to execute predefined prompts, enabling full automation of routine operational tasks.

sections.lead: Integrating verification loops, multi-cloud management, and background routines establishes a system for reliable AI agent task delegation.

bullets.label: Integrated System
bullets.text: The framework integrates verification loops, multi-cloud management strategies, and automated background routines.

bullets.label: Reliable Delegation
bullets.text: This system allows for significant delegation of work to Cloud with high reliability and confidence.

bullets.label: Human Focus
bullets.text: Humans can ultimately allocate their attention and time to the most critical and creative aspects of software engineering.
